topics.meaning: The scientific method is a step-by-step way of asking a question and finding its answer through observation and testing.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Science is not only about memorising facts or doing experiments. It is a way of thinking that helps us find answers to our questions. We begin by observing something interesting that we do not understand. This makes us wonder and ask a question about it. We then make a guess about a possible answer. Next we test this guess through experiments or more observations, and finally we analyse the results to see if our guess actually answers the question. If it does not, we make a new guess and test again. This careful, step-by-step process is called the scientific method, and anyone who follows it is working like a scientist.
topics.easyExplanation: The scientific method is just a smart way of solving a puzzle: notice something, ask why, guess the answer, test the guess, and check if you were right.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We notice something interesting or confusing in the world around us, like a pen that suddenly stops writing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Ask a question
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Our observation makes us wonder, so we form a clear question such as 'Why did my pen stop writing?'

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make a guess
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We think of a possible answer, called a guess or hypothesis, for example 'Maybe the ink is finished.'

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test the guess
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We test the guess by experimenting or observing, like opening the pen to check the ink refill.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Analyse the result
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We study what we found. If the guess was right, we have our answer; if not, we make a new guess and test again.

examples: A cook wondering why the dal spilled out of the cooker and checking if there was too much water is using the scientific method.
An electrician finding out why a bulb does not light by checking the bulb and then the switch is thinking like a scientist.

importantKeywords.definition: A step-by-step process of observing, questioning, guessing, testing and analysing to find answers.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Checking why a torch does not glow by testing the cells, then the bulb, follows the scientific method.

importantKeywords.definition: Carefully noticing things using our senses.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Seeing that ice melts in your warm hand is an observation.

importantKeywords.definition: A possible answer or guess that we test.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Guessing that a plant grew taller because it got more sunlight is a hypothesis.

topics.quickSummary: The scientific method is a step-by-step way of finding answers: observe, question, guess, test and analyse. Anyone who follows it is working like a scientist.
revisionNotes: Science is a way of thinking, not just facts.
Steps: observe -> question -> guess -> test -> analyse.
If a guess fails, make a new one and test again.
Anyone following these steps works like a scientist.

topics.meaning: Science is all around us, and we use scientific thinking in many ordinary daily activities without even realising it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We often think science happens only in laboratories, but we actually use it every day. Whenever we ask 'how' or 'why' about something and try to find the answer, we are doing science. The whole book invites us to be curious about everything on Earth and even beyond, such as the Sun, the Moon and the millions of stars. The most important quality for learning science is curiosity, observing our surroundings keenly and asking questions. Science is also rarely done alone; scientists across the world work together in teams, so we too can discover things better by working with our friends.
topics.easyExplanation: Science is not stuck inside labs. Every time you ask 'why' and try to find out, you are doing science, especially when you explore it with friends.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Be curious
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Curiosity is the starting point of all science. Wondering about the world makes us ask questions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe keenly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Watching our surroundings carefully helps us notice details others might miss.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Ask how and why
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Turning curiosity into clear 'how' and 'why' questions sets us on the path to answers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Explore together
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Science is rarely done alone; sharing and working with friends makes discovery easier and more fun.

examples: Wondering why the sky changes colour at sunset and trying to find out is doing science.
A group of friends together figuring out why a paper boat sinks are working like a team of scientists.

importantKeywords.definition: A strong wish to know or learn about something.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Asking why the Moon changes shape each night shows curiosity.

importantKeywords.definition: The act of asking questions and investigating to learn.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Investigating why plants near a window grow towards it is inquiry.

topics.quickSummary: Science is part of everyday life. By staying curious, observing closely, asking how and why, and exploring together, we all act as scientists.
revisionNotes: Science is everywhere, not just in labs.
Curiosity is the key quality for learning science.
Asking 'how' and 'why' leads to discoveries.
Science is often done together in teams.

topics.topic: Diversity in Plants and Animals Around Us
topics.meaning: Diversity means the great variety of different plants and animals found in nature around us.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look around in a park, forest or field, we see a huge variety of living things: grasses, bushes, large trees, birds, butterflies, monkeys and many more. Each kind of plant and animal is different in its shape, size, colour, the sounds it makes and the way it lives. For example, each bird has its own unique chirp. This wonderful variety of living things in nature is called diversity. Observing this diversity carefully, without disturbing the creatures, helps us understand and respect all forms of life.
topics.easyExplanation: Diversity simply means 'lots of different kinds'. Nature is full of many different plants and animals, each special in its own way.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Look around
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Wherever we go, we find many living things, from tiny grasses to tall trees and from butterflies to monkeys.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice the differences
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants and animals differ in shape, size, colour, sound and habits. Even two birds can have very different chirps.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Call it diversity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This great variety among living things is what we call diversity in the living world.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe with respect
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We should observe plants and animals quietly, without harming or disturbing them.

examples: A single garden may have roses, mango trees, ants, sparrows and squirrels all living together.
Different birds like the koel, crow and sparrow each make their own distinct sound.

importantKeywords.definition: The variety of different living things found in nature.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A forest with many kinds of trees, birds and insects shows diversity.

importantKeywords.definition: The natural place where a plant or animal lives.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A pond is the habitat of a fish.

importantKeywords.definition: Things that grow, breathe, feed, reproduce and respond, such as plants and animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A dog and a mango tree are living things.

topics.quickSummary: Nature shows great diversity, a huge variety of plants and animals that differ in shape, size, colour, sound and habits, and we should observe them with respect.
revisionNotes: Diversity = variety of living things.
Plants and animals differ in shape, size, colour, sound and habits.
Each bird has a unique chirp.
Observe living things without disturbing them.

topics.topic: How to Group Plants and Animals
topics.meaning: Grouping means sorting plants and animals into sets based on shared features so they are easier to study.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Because there are so many living things, scientists group them to study them more easily. Plants can be grouped by their height and the kind of stem they have into herbs (small and soft), shrubs (medium with many woody branches near the ground) and trees (tall with a thick, hard trunk). They can also be grouped by features like the type of leaves or roots. Animals can be grouped in many ways, such as by where they live (land, water or air), what they eat (plant-eaters, animal-eaters or both), or how they move (walk, fly, swim or crawl). Grouping things with similar features is called classification.
topics.easyExplanation: Just like we sort our books or clothes into groups, we sort plants and animals into groups based on what they have in common.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose a feature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pick a feature to sort by, such as a plant's height or where an animal lives.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group plants
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: By height and stem, plants are herbs (small, soft), shrubs (medium, woody, bushy) or trees (tall, thick trunk).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group animals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Animals can be grouped by habitat (land, water, air), food habits, or how they move.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Call it classification
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sorting living things into groups based on shared features is called classification, and it makes study easier.

examples: Tulsi is a herb, rose is a shrub, and mango is a tree.
A fish is grouped as a water animal, a sparrow as an air animal, and a cow as a land animal.

importantKeywords.definition: A small plant with a soft, green stem.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A coriander plant is a herb.

importantKeywords.definition: A medium plant with many hard, woody branches near the ground.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A rose plant is a shrub.

importantKeywords.definition: A tall plant with a single thick, hard, woody trunk.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A banyan is a tree.

importantKeywords.definition: Sorting living things into groups based on shared features.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Putting all water animals together is classification.

topics.quickSummary: We group the huge variety of living things to study them easily: plants as herbs, shrubs and trees, and animals by habitat, food or movement. This sorting is called classification.
revisionNotes: Grouping makes study easier.
Plants by height/stem: herbs, shrubs, trees.
Animals grouped by habitat, food habits or movement.
Sorting by shared features = classification.

topics.topic: Plants and Animals in Different Habitats
topics.meaning: A habitat is the natural home of a living thing, and plants and animals have special features that help them survive in their habitat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Different plants and animals live in different surroundings called habitats, such as forests, deserts, ponds, oceans and mountains. Living things have special features, called adaptations, that help them survive in their particular habitat. For example, a cactus in a hot, dry desert has thick stems to store water and spines instead of leaves to reduce water loss. A fish has a streamlined body and gills to live and breathe in water. The conditions of a habitat, like the amount of water, sunlight and temperature, decide which plants and animals can live there.
topics.easyExplanation: A habitat is an animal's or plant's home. Living things have special body features that fit their home, like a camel made for the desert or a fish made for water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know the habitat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A habitat is the natural surroundings where a plant or animal lives, such as a desert, pond or forest.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Match features to home
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living things have special features, called adaptations, suited to their habitat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See desert examples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A cactus stores water in its thick stem and has spines instead of leaves to save water in the dry desert.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See water examples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A fish has a smooth, streamlined body and gills to swim and breathe underwater.

examples: A camel has long legs and can go days without water, helping it live in the hot desert.
A lotus grows in ponds with its roots in mud and leaves floating on water.

importantKeywords.definition: The natural place where a plant or animal lives.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A desert is the habitat of a camel.

importantKeywords.definition: A special feature that helps a living thing survive in its habitat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A cactus's spines are an adaptation to save water.

importantKeywords.definition: Living in water.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A fish is an aquatic animal.

importantKeywords.definition: Living on land.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A tiger is a terrestrial animal.

topics.quickSummary: Plants and animals live in different habitats and have special adaptations, like a cactus's water-storing stem or a fish's gills, that help them survive where they live.
revisionNotes: Habitat = natural home of a living thing.
Adaptations are features that aid survival.
Cactus: thick stem stores water, spines reduce water loss.
Fish: streamlined body and gills for water life.

chapters.chapter: Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body

topics.meaning: We eat a variety of foods made from plants and animals, and these foods differ from one region to another.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Across India, people eat many different kinds of food. What we eat depends on where we live, what grows there, and our family habits. The foods we eat come from two main sources: plants and animals. From plants we get grains, fruits, vegetables, pulses, oils and spices, while from animals we get milk, eggs, meat and honey. A single dish, like a vegetable curry with rice, can have ingredients from many plants. Cooking practices and food choices have also changed over time as new ingredients and methods have spread from place to place.
topics.easyExplanation: We eat lots of different foods, and they all come from either plants or animals. What people eat changes from place to place.

stepByStepExplanation.title: List your foods
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Think about the meals you eat in a day, from breakfast to dinner, including snacks.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the source
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each food comes from a plant (like rice, dal, vegetables) or an animal (like milk, eggs, honey).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice regional variety
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People in different regions eat different staple foods depending on what grows nearby.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See that food changes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cooking practices and food choices change over time as ingredients and ideas travel between places.

examples: Idli and sambar are common in the south, while makki di roti and sarson da saag are popular in Punjab.
Milk and honey come from animals, while rice and apples come from plants.

importantKeywords.definition: The different food items used to make a dish.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rice, dal and spices are the ingredients of khichdi.

importantKeywords.definition: Food that comes from plants.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Wheat is a plant-source food.

importantKeywords.definition: Food that comes from animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Eggs are an animal-source food.

topics.quickSummary: We eat a wide variety of foods that come from plants and animals; food habits differ by region and change over time.
revisionNotes: Food comes from plants and animals.
What we eat depends on region and family habits.
One dish can use many ingredients.
Food and cooking practices change over time.

topics.meaning: Food is made up of nutrients, mainly carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins and minerals, plus dietary fibre and water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The food we eat is made up of useful substances called nutrients, each doing a special job in our body. Carbohydrates and fats give us energy, and fats also store energy. Proteins help our body grow and repair itself, and are called body-building foods. Vitamins and minerals protect us from diseases and keep the body working well; they are called protective foods. Besides nutrients, food also contains dietary fibre, also called roughage, which helps in digestion, and water, which is needed for all body activities. A good meal should contain all of these components.
topics.easyExplanation: Food is a mix of helpful parts called nutrients. Some give energy, some build the body, and some protect it. Food also has fibre and water that keep us healthy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Energy-givers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Carbohydrates and fats provide energy; fats also store energy for later use.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Body-builders
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Proteins help the body grow and repair damaged parts, so they are body-building foods.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Protectors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Vitamins and minerals protect the body from diseases and keep it working properly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fibre and water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dietary fibre (roughage) helps digestion, and water is needed for all body activities.

examples: Rice, potato and bread are rich in carbohydrates for energy.
Pulses, milk and eggs are rich in proteins for growth.

importantKeywords.definition: A useful substance in food that the body needs.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Protein is a nutrient found in dal.

importantKeywords.definition: A nutrient that gives the body energy.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rice is full of carbohydrates.

importantKeywords.definition: A nutrient that helps the body grow and repair.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Eggs are rich in protein.

importantKeywords.definition: Dietary fibre that helps in digestion.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Whole fruits and vegetables give us roughage.

topics.quickSummary: Food contains nutrients (carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins, minerals), plus fibre and water. Each component does a special job: energy, body-building or protection.
revisionNotes: Nutrients: carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins, minerals.
Carbohydrates and fats give energy.
Proteins build and repair the body.
Vitamins and minerals protect; fibre aids digestion; water is essential.

topics.meaning: Simple tests using special substances can tell us whether a food contains starch, fats or proteins.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can find out which nutrients are present in a food by doing simple tests. To test for starch (a carbohydrate), we add a few drops of dilute iodine solution to the food; if it turns blue-black, starch is present. To test for fats, we rub the food on a piece of paper; if it leaves an oily patch that lets light through, fats are present. To test for proteins, we use copper sulphate and caustic soda solutions; a violet or purple colour shows that proteins are present. These tests help us understand what our food is made of.
topics.easyExplanation: We can test food with simple tricks: iodine turns blue-black with starch, an oily mark on paper shows fat, and a violet colour shows protein.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for starch
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add dilute iodine to the food. A blue-black colour means starch is present.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for fats
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rub the food on paper. An oily patch that lets light through means fats are present.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for proteins
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add copper sulphate and caustic soda solutions. A violet colour means proteins are present.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the result
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The colour change or oily mark tells us which nutrient the food contains.

examples: A drop of iodine on boiled potato turns blue-black, showing it has starch.
A piece of puri leaves an oily mark on paper, showing it contains fat.

importantKeywords.definition: A test where iodine turns blue-black in the presence of starch.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Iodine on bread turns blue-black.

importantKeywords.definition: A type of carbohydrate found in many foods.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rice and potato are rich in starch.

importantKeywords.definition: Energy-rich nutrients that leave an oily mark on paper.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Butter and oil are fats.

topics.quickSummary: Simple tests reveal a food's nutrients: iodine turns blue-black with starch, an oily paper patch shows fats, and a violet colour with copper sulphate shows proteins.
revisionNotes: Iodine + starch -> blue-black.
Oily patch on paper -> fats present.
Copper sulphate + caustic soda + protein -> violet colour.
Tests show what nutrients a food has.

topics.meaning: A balanced diet is one that contains all the nutrients, fibre and water in the right amounts needed for good health.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Our body needs different nutrients in the right amounts to stay healthy, and no single food contains all of them. A diet that gives us all the nutrients (carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins and minerals), along with dietary fibre and water, in the correct quantities is called a balanced diet. The right amount of food varies from person to person depending on age, gender and the kind of work they do. Eating too little or too much, or missing some nutrients, can lead to deficiency diseases or poor health. Eating a variety of foods mindfully helps us get a balanced diet.
topics.easyExplanation: A balanced diet means eating the right mix of all nutrients, plus fibre and water, so your body gets everything it needs to stay healthy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know the need
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The body needs all nutrients, but no single food has them all, so we must eat a variety.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define balanced diet
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A balanced diet provides all nutrients, fibre and water in the right amounts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Adjust to the person
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: How much food a person needs depends on their age, gender and activity level.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Avoid imbalance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Too little, too much, or missing nutrients can cause deficiency diseases and poor health.

examples: A meal of roti, dal, vegetables, curd and a fruit gives a good mix of nutrients.
A growing child playing sports needs more food than an adult doing light desk work.

importantKeywords.definition: A diet with all nutrients, fibre and water in the right amounts.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Roti, dal, salad and fruit together make a balanced meal.

importantKeywords.definition: An illness caused by the lack of a nutrient in the diet.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Lack of vitamin C can cause scurvy.

importantKeywords.definition: Eating thoughtfully, choosing healthy food in the right amount.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Choosing fruit over too many sweets is mindful eating.

topics.quickSummary: A balanced diet supplies all nutrients, fibre and water in the right amounts; the amount needed varies by person, and an unbalanced diet can cause deficiency diseases.
revisionNotes: Balanced diet = all nutrients + fibre + water in right amounts.
No single food has everything; eat variety.
Needs vary with age, gender and activity.
Imbalance causes deficiency diseases.

topics.meaning: Millets are traditional cereal grains that are rich in nutrients and good for both our health and the environment.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Millets are a group of small-seeded cereal grains, such as jowar (sorghum), bajra (pearl millet) and ragi (finger millet), that have been grown in India for thousands of years. They are highly nutritious, being rich in proteins, dietary fibre and minerals, which is why they are often called nutri-cereals. Millets can grow in dry areas with little water and poor soil, so they are good for the environment and for farmers. Because of their many benefits, the year 2023 was celebrated as the International Year of Millets to encourage people to include them in their diet.
topics.easyExplanation: Millets like jowar, bajra and ragi are healthy old grains packed with nutrients. They grow easily with little water, which is great for farmers and the planet.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Meet the millets
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Millets are small grains like jowar, bajra and ragi, grown in India for thousands of years.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See their nutrition
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: They are rich in proteins, fibre and minerals, earning the name nutri-cereals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note their hardiness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Millets grow in dry regions with little water and poor soil, helping farmers and the environment.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Celebrate them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 2023 was the International Year of Millets, encouraging people worldwide to eat more millets.

examples: Ragi can be used to make porridge, dosa or laddoo.
Bajra rotis are a common food in the dry regions of Rajasthan.

importantKeywords.definition: Small-seeded nutritious cereal grains such as jowar, bajra and ragi.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Ragi is a millet used to make healthy porridge.

importantKeywords.definition: Another name for millets because they are rich in nutrients.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Bajra is called a nutri-cereal.

importantKeywords.definition: A grain used as food, such as wheat, rice or millet.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Jowar is a cereal.

topics.quickSummary: Millets like jowar, bajra and ragi are nutrient-rich nutri-cereals that grow easily in dry areas; 2023 was the International Year of Millets to promote them.
revisionNotes: Millets: jowar, bajra, ragi and more.
Rich in protein, fibre and minerals (nutri-cereals).
Grow with little water in poor soil.
2023 = International Year of Millets.

topics.topic: Food Miles: From Farm to Our Plate
topics.meaning: Food miles are the distance that food travels from where it is grown to where it is eaten.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The food on our plate often travels a long way before reaching us, from the farm where it is grown, through markets and transport, to our home. The distance that food travels from the farm to our plate is called its food miles. Food grown far away has to be transported over long distances, which uses fuel and can cause pollution, and the food may also lose some freshness. Eating food that is grown locally, and that is in season, means fewer food miles, fresher food, and less harm to the environment. This is part of eating mindfully.
topics.easyExplanation: Food miles is how far your food travels to reach you. Eating local, seasonal food cuts down this travel, keeps food fresh and helps the planet.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Trace the journey
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food travels from the farm, through markets and transport, to our plate.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define food miles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food miles is the total distance food travels from where it is grown to where it is eaten.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See the cost
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Long-distance food uses more fuel, causes more pollution and may be less fresh.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose local
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Eating local and seasonal food reduces food miles, keeps food fresh and protects the environment.

examples: An apple grown in your own state travels fewer food miles than one brought from another country.
Buying vegetables from a nearby farm means fresher food and less transport pollution.

importantKeywords.definition: The distance food travels from farm to plate.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Locally grown spinach has low food miles.

importantKeywords.definition: Food grown near where it is eaten.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Vegetables from a nearby village are local food.

importantKeywords.definition: Food that naturally grows in a particular season.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mangoes in summer are seasonal food.

topics.quickSummary: Food miles is the distance food travels from farm to plate; choosing local, seasonal food lowers food miles, keeps food fresh and reduces pollution.
revisionNotes: Food miles = distance food travels to reach us.
Long travel uses fuel and causes pollution.
Local, seasonal food has fewer food miles.
Eating local is fresher and greener.

topics.meaning: Magnetic materials are those attracted by a magnet, while non-magnetic materials are not attracted by a magnet.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A magnet is an object that can attract certain materials towards it. Materials that are attracted by a magnet are called magnetic materials, for example iron, nickel and cobalt. Materials that are not attracted by a magnet are called non-magnetic materials, for example wood, plastic, paper, cloth and rubber. We can test whether a material is magnetic by bringing a magnet close to it and seeing if it is pulled towards the magnet. Magnets come in different shapes such as bar magnets, horseshoe magnets and ring magnets.
topics.easyExplanation: Some things stick to a magnet (magnetic, like iron) and some do not (non-magnetic, like plastic or wood). You can check by holding a magnet near them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know a magnet
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A magnet is an object that attracts certain materials towards it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify magnetic materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Materials pulled by a magnet, like iron, nickel and cobalt, are magnetic materials.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify non-magnetic materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Materials not pulled by a magnet, like wood, plastic and paper, are non-magnetic materials.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Bring a magnet near an object; if it is attracted, the material is magnetic.

examples: A magnet picks up iron nails and pins, showing they are magnetic.
A magnet does not attract a plastic ruler or a wooden pencil, showing they are non-magnetic.

importantKeywords.definition: An object that attracts magnetic materials like iron.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A fridge magnet sticks to an iron door.

importantKeywords.definition: A material that is attracted by a magnet.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Iron is a magnetic material.

importantKeywords.definition: A material that is not attracted by a magnet.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Plastic is a non-magnetic material.

topics.quickSummary: Magnets attract magnetic materials like iron, nickel and cobalt, but not non-magnetic materials like wood and plastic; we test this by bringing a magnet close.
revisionNotes: Magnet attracts certain materials.
Magnetic: iron, nickel, cobalt.
Non-magnetic: wood, plastic, paper, cloth, rubber.
Test by bringing a magnet near the object.

topics.meaning: Every magnet has two poles, the north pole and the south pole, where its attracting power is the strongest.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a magnet is dipped in iron filings, the filings stick mostly at the two ends of the magnet. These two ends, where the pull of the magnet is strongest, are called the poles of the magnet. Every magnet has two poles: a north-seeking pole (north pole) and a south-seeking pole (south pole). The poles always come in a pair; if a magnet is broken into pieces, each piece still has both a north and a south pole. The poles are usually marked N and S on the magnet.
topics.easyExplanation: A magnet's pulling power is strongest at its two ends, called the north pole and south pole. Even a broken magnet keeps both poles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dip in iron filings
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When a magnet is placed in iron filings, most filings cling to its two ends.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the poles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These two ends, where the magnet's pull is strongest, are called its poles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the poles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every magnet has a north pole (north-seeking) and a south pole (south-seeking).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note poles stay in pairs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If a magnet breaks, each piece still has both a north and a south pole.

examples: Iron filings stick thickly at both ends of a bar magnet but hardly at the middle.
Breaking a bar magnet in two gives two smaller magnets, each with an N and an S pole.

topics.meaning: A freely suspended magnet always comes to rest pointing in the north-south direction, which helps us find directions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a bar magnet is hung freely so that it can rotate, it always comes to rest pointing in the north-south direction. The end that points north is the north pole, and the end that points south is the south pole. Because of this property, magnets have been used since ancient times to find directions. A small device called a compass uses a tiny magnetic needle that always points north-south, helping travellers, sailors and others find their way.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The end pointing north is the north pole; the end pointing south is the south pole.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use a compass
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A compass uses a small magnetic needle that points north-south to show directions.

topics.meaning: Like poles of two magnets repel each other, while unlike poles attract each other.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When two magnets are brought close, they affect each other depending on which poles face each other. If the north pole of one magnet faces the north pole of another (or south facing south), the magnets push each other away; this is called repulsion. If the north pole of one faces the south pole of another, the magnets pull towards each other; this is called attraction. So the rule is: like poles repel and unlike poles attract. Repulsion is a sure test to check whether an object is really a magnet, because only two magnets can repel each other.
topics.easyExplanation: Two magnets play push and pull: same poles push apart (repel), opposite poles pull together (attract). If two things push apart, both are magnets.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Bring magnets close
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Take two magnets and bring their poles near each other.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same poles repel
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: North-north or south-south facing poles push each other away. This is repulsion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Opposite poles attract
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: North facing south pulls the magnets together. This is attraction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use repulsion as a test
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only two magnets can repel each other, so repulsion proves an object is a magnet.

examples: Two north poles brought together push each other apart.
The north pole of one magnet sticks firmly to the south pole of another.

importantKeywords.definition: The pull between unlike poles of two magnets.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A north pole and a south pole attract each other.

importantKeywords.definition: The push between like poles of two magnets.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Two south poles repel each other.

importantKeywords.definition: Poles of the same kind, such as two north poles.

revisionNotes: Like poles repel (push apart).
Unlike poles attract (pull together).

topics.meaning: Magnets can be made, can lose their magnetism, and are used in many useful ways in daily life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can make our own magnet from an iron object by stroking it many times in the same direction with one pole of a bar magnet; this makes the iron behave like a magnet. Magnets can also lose their magnetism if they are heated strongly, hammered, or dropped roughly, so they must be stored carefully. Magnets are used in many everyday things, such as in pin holders, fridge doors, pencil boxes, electric bells, and in devices like loudspeakers and computers. Magnets should be kept away from items like mobile phones, ATM cards and televisions, which they can damage.
topics.easyExplanation: You can make a magnet by stroking iron with a magnet, and magnets can lose their power if heated or dropped. Magnets are used in toys, gadgets and many tools.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make a magnet

stepByStepExplanation.title: Store carefully
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep magnets in pairs with unlike poles together and away from delicate devices.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use magnets
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Magnets are used in pin holders, fridge doors, electric bells, loudspeakers and many gadgets.

examples: Stroking an iron nail with a bar magnet many times turns the nail into a magnet.
A magnet in a pencil box keeps the lid shut and holds pins together.

topics.meaning: Measurement means comparing an unknown quantity with a known fixed quantity called a unit.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Measurement is a part of our daily life, whether we are buying cloth, cooking food or building a house. To measure something means to compare it with a known fixed amount called a unit. The number of times the unit is contained in the quantity gives the measurement. In the past, people used body parts like the hand span, cubit (elbow to fingertip) and foot to measure length. The problem was that these measurements differed from person to person, because everyone's body parts are of different sizes, leading to confusion. This is why fixed, standard units became necessary.
topics.easyExplanation: Measuring means comparing something with a fixed amount called a unit. Long ago people used body parts, but since everyone's body is different, the results did not match.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare with a unit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Measuring means comparing a quantity with a known fixed amount called a unit.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The measurement is how many times the unit fits into the quantity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recall old methods
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People once measured using body parts like the hand span, cubit and foot.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See the problem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Body parts differ from person to person, so these measurements were not reliable.

examples: Measuring a table as '5 hand spans' long depends on whose hand is used.
Buying 3 metres of cloth gives the same length everywhere because the metre is a fixed unit.

topics.meaning: Standard units are fixed units of measurement that are the same everywhere, like the metre for length.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To avoid the confusion caused by body-based measurements, the world agreed on fixed units that are the same for everyone. These are called standard units, and the system used worldwide is the International System of Units (SI units). The standard unit of length is the metre (m). Smaller lengths are measured in centimetres (cm) and millimetres (mm), while larger lengths use the kilometre (km). The relations are: 1 m = 100 cm, 1 cm = 10 mm, and 1 km = 1000 m. Using standard units means a measurement made by one person means exactly the same to everyone else.
topics.easyExplanation: Standard units are fixed and the same everywhere, so measurements always match. The metre is the standard unit of length, with cm, mm and km for smaller and larger lengths.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Need standard units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fixed units that are the same for everyone remove the confusion of body-based measuring.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the SI system
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The world uses the International System of Units (SI), with the metre as the standard unit of length.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Learn the smaller/larger units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Length is also measured in millimetres, centimetres and kilometres.

examples: The length of a pencil is measured in centimetres, while the distance between cities is measured in kilometres.
A height of 1 metre means the same thing to a person in Delhi and a person in Chennai.

topics.meaning: To measure length correctly we must use the scale properly, avoiding broken ends and reading at eye level.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When measuring length with a ruler or scale, we must follow the correct method to get an accurate value. If the zero mark of the scale is broken or worn, we should start measuring from another full mark (like 1 cm) and then subtract that reading from the final reading. The eye must be placed exactly in front of the point being read, looking straight down, to avoid an error called parallax. The scale should also be placed right along the object being measured. Following these steps gives a correct measurement.
topics.easyExplanation: To measure length right: avoid the broken end of the scale, line the scale up with the object, and look straight down at the mark, not from an angle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the scale's end
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If the zero end is broken, start from a clear full mark and subtract it from the final reading.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place the scale correctly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Lay the scale right along the object so its markings touch the length being measured.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read at eye level
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place your eye straight in front of the reading point and look down vertically.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Avoid parallax error
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Looking from an angle gives a wrong reading; reading straight on avoids this parallax error.

examples: If a ruler's zero edge is broken, measuring from the 1 cm mark and subtracting 1 cm gives the correct length.
Reading the scale from the side instead of straight above gives a wrong value.

importantKeywords.simpleExample: Measure from the zero mark when it is not broken.

topics.quickSummary: To measure length correctly, avoid the broken end of the scale, align it with the object, and read straight on at eye level to avoid parallax error.

topics.topic: Measuring the Length of a Curved Line
topics.meaning: The length of a curved line can be measured using a thread or string and then a straight scale.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A straight scale cannot directly measure a curved or bent line. To measure such a length, we take a thread and place it carefully along the curved line, following all its bends, and mark the start and end points on the thread. We then straighten the thread along a scale and read its length. This gives the length of the curved line. This simple method lets us measure things like the curved edge of a leaf or the boundary of a map.
topics.easyExplanation: You can't measure a curvy line with a straight ruler. Instead, lay a thread along the curve, then straighten the thread against a ruler to read the length.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Lay a thread
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place a thread along the curved line, following all its bends carefully.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mark the ends
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mark the points on the thread where the curved line starts and ends.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Straighten the thread
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Stretch the thread straight along a scale.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the length
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Read the distance between the two marks on the scale to get the curved line's length.

examples: Measuring the curved boundary of a leaf using a thread and then a ruler.
Finding the length of a winding road on a map using a piece of string.

importantKeywords.definition: A line that is not straight but bends.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The edge of a circle is a curved line.

importantKeywords.definition: Using a thread to measure a curved length, then a scale.

topics.meaning: The position of an object is described by stating its distance and direction from a fixed reference point.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To describe where an object is, we need a fixed point to compare it with, called a reference point. The position of the object is then given by its distance and direction from this reference point. For example, saying a shop is '100 metres to the east of the school' uses the school as the reference point. Without a reference point, we cannot clearly describe a position. The reference point chosen can be different for different observers, so it must be stated clearly.
topics.easyExplanation: To say where something is, you pick a fixed spot (a reference point) and give the distance and direction from it, like '50 metres north of the gate'.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose a reference point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pick a fixed, known point to describe position from, such as a school or a tree.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Give the distance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State how far the object is from the reference point.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Give the direction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: State in which direction the object lies from the reference point.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State it clearly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because different people may pick different reference points, always mention the one used.

examples: A bus stop that is '200 metres south of the temple' uses the temple as the reference point.
Your house being 'third from the corner' describes its position using the corner as a reference.

topics.meaning: An object is in motion if its position changes with time, and at rest if its position does not change.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some things around us stay in one place while others move. If the position of an object changes with respect to its surroundings as time passes, the object is said to be in motion. If its position does not change, the object is said to be at rest. Whether an object is at rest or in motion depends on the reference point chosen; for example, a passenger sitting in a moving bus is at rest with respect to the bus but in motion with respect to the road. Motion is therefore described relative to a reference point.
topics.easyExplanation: If something's position changes over time, it is moving (in motion); if it stays put, it is at rest. Whether it moves depends on what you compare it with.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Watch the position
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note an object's position and check if it changes as time passes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define motion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If the position changes with time, the object is in motion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define rest
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If the position does not change with time, the object is at rest.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note it is relative
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Whether an object is at rest or moving depends on the reference point chosen.

examples: A car moving on the road changes its position, so it is in motion.
A passenger in a moving train is at rest relative to the train but in motion relative to the ground.

importantKeywords.definition: A change in the position of an object with time.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A flying bird is in motion.

importantKeywords.definition: The state of an object whose position does not change with time.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A parked car is at rest.

importantKeywords.definition: Described with respect to something else.

topics.meaning: Motion can be of different types, such as straight-line (linear), circular, and to-and-fro (oscillatory) motion.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Objects move in different ways, giving us different types of motion. In straight-line or linear motion, an object moves along a straight path, like a car on a straight road or a marching soldier. In circular motion, an object moves along a circular path, like the tip of a fan blade or the Moon around the Earth. In oscillatory or to-and-fro motion, an object moves back and forth about a fixed point, like a swing or the pendulum of a clock. Some objects show more than one type of motion at the same time.
topics.easyExplanation: Things move in different ways: in a straight line (linear), in a circle (circular), or back and forth (oscillatory) like a swing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Circular motion

stepByStepExplanation.title: Oscillatory motion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Back-and-forth movement about a fixed point, like a swing or a pendulum.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Combined motion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some objects show more than one type of motion together, like a rolling ball that moves forward and spins.

examples: A pendulum of a wall clock shows oscillatory motion.
The hands of a clock show circular motion, while a sprinter on a straight track shows linear motion.

topics.meaning: Objects around us are made of one or more materials, and the same object can be made from different materials.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Everything we see around us, like a chair, a spoon or a bottle, is an object, and every object is made up of some material. A single object can be made of more than one material; for example, a pencil has wood and graphite. Also, the same kind of object can be made from different materials; for example, a glass can be made of glass, plastic, steel or even clay. We choose a material for an object based on the properties we need, such as strength, shininess or the ability to hold water.
topics.easyExplanation: Everything around us is an object made of materials. One object can have many materials, and the same object can be made from different materials depending on what we need.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Spot the object
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Anything we can see and touch, like a bottle or a chair, is an object.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find its material
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every object is made of one or more materials, such as wood, metal or plastic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See multiple materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A single object can be made of more than one material, like a pencil of wood and graphite.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See material choice
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The same object can be made of different materials, chosen for the properties we need.

topics.meaning: Materials can be grouped on the basis of their properties such as appearance, hardness and whether they dissolve in water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Since there are so many materials, we group them to study them easily. We can group materials based on properties like their appearance (shiny or dull), how hard or soft they are, whether they float or sink in water, whether they are transparent, translucent or opaque, and whether they dissolve in water. Grouping materials by their properties helps us understand them and choose the right material for a job. The way we group them depends on which property we look at.
topics.easyExplanation: We sort materials into groups based on their qualities, like shiny or dull, hard or soft, floats or sinks, see-through or not, and dissolves or not.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Choose a property to group by, such as appearance, hardness or solubility.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Materials can be shiny (lustrous) like metals, or dull like wood.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group by hardness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Materials can be hard like iron or soft like cotton.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group by behaviour in water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Materials may float or sink, and may or may not dissolve in water.

examples: Iron and gold are shiny, while chalk and wood are dull.

topics.meaning: Materials have different properties like lustre, hardness, solubility, transparency, and the ability to float or sink.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Each material has its own set of properties that make it suitable for certain uses. Lustre is the shine of a material; metals are usually lustrous. Hardness tells how easily a material can be scratched or pressed; a diamond is very hard while cotton is soft. Solubility tells whether a material dissolves in water, like sugar and salt do. Transparency describes how much light passes through: transparent materials (clear glass) let light through fully, translucent materials (oily paper) partly, and opaque materials (wood) not at all. Materials may also float or sink in water. These properties decide how we use a material.
topics.easyExplanation: Materials differ in shine, hardness, whether they dissolve, how much you can see through them, and whether they float. These qualities decide their uses.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Lustre
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shiny materials have lustre; most metals are lustrous, while non-metals are usually dull.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Hardness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hard materials resist scratching and pressing; soft materials are easily pressed.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Transparency

examples: A diamond is extremely hard, while clay is soft and easy to mould.

topics.meaning: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space, and it exists mainly in three states: solid, liquid and gas.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: All materials around us are made of matter. Matter is anything that has mass (a certain amount of stuff) and occupies space (takes up room). Matter exists in three main states: solids, liquids and gases. Solids have a fixed shape and a fixed volume, like a stone. Liquids have a fixed volume but take the shape of their container, like water. Gases have neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume and fill up any space available, like air. Recognising these states helps us understand how different materials behave.
topics.easyExplanation: Matter is anything that has weight and takes up space. It comes in three forms: solid (fixed shape), liquid (takes container's shape), and gas (spreads everywhere).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define matter
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solids
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solids have a fixed shape and a fixed volume, like a book or a stone.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Liquids
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Liquids have a fixed volume but take the shape of their container, like water or milk.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Gases

topics.meaning: Our sense of touch can tell us roughly whether something is hot or cold, but it is not always reliable.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We often judge whether things are hot or cold by touching them. However, our sense of touch can fool us. If we put one hand in warm water and the other in cold water, and then put both into the same lukewarm water, the cold hand feels it as warm while the warm hand feels it as cold. This shows that our feeling of hot and cold is only a rough guess and can be misleading. To measure how hot or cold something really is, we need an accurate measure called temperature, found using a thermometer.
topics.easyExplanation: Touch can tell you if something feels hot or cold, but it can trick you. To know the real hotness, we measure temperature with a thermometer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Judge by touch
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We often decide if something is hot or cold by touching it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Try the two-hand test
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hands kept in warm and cold water feel the same lukewarm water differently.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See touch is unreliable

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To know exactly how hot or cold something is, we measure its temperature with a thermometer.

topics.meaning: Temperature is a measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of an object, measured in degrees Celsius.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Temperature is the proper scientific measure of how hot or cold something is. It is usually measured in degrees Celsius, written as the symbol degree C. For example, the normal temperature of a healthy human body is about 37 degrees Celsius. Water freezes into ice at 0 degrees Celsius and boils into steam at 100 degrees Celsius. Knowing the temperature is important in daily life, in cooking, in weather reports, and especially in checking the health of a person, because a high body temperature shows fever.
topics.easyExplanation: Temperature tells exactly how hot or cold something is, measured in degrees Celsius. Body temperature is about 37 degrees C; water freezes at 0 and boils at 100.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define temperature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Temperature is the measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of an object.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use degrees Celsius
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Temperature is commonly measured in degrees Celsius (degree C).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know key values
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Normal body temperature is about 37 degree C; water freezes at 0 degree C and boils at 100 degree C.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See its importance

topics.meaning: Temperature is measured using a thermometer; common types are the clinical thermometer and the laboratory thermometer.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A thermometer is a device used to measure temperature. A clinical thermometer is used to measure the temperature of the human body and is marked from about 35 degree C to 42 degree C, since body temperature stays in this range. A laboratory thermometer is used to measure the temperature of other things, like liquids, and covers a wider range, often from -10 degree C to 110 degree C. Older thermometers used mercury, but because mercury is harmful, digital thermometers are now preferred for safety. A thermometer should be read carefully with the eye level with the top of the liquid thread.
topics.easyExplanation: We measure temperature with a thermometer. A clinical one checks body heat; a lab one checks other things over a wider range. Digital thermometers are now safer to use.

chapters.chapter: A Journey through States of Water

topics.meaning: Evaporation is the slow change of water from its liquid state into water vapour (a gas) at any temperature.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When wet clothes are hung out to dry or a puddle dries up in the Sun, the water seems to disappear. In fact, the liquid water slowly changes into an invisible gas called water vapour and mixes into the air. This slow change of water from liquid to vapour, which happens at any temperature, is called evaporation. Evaporation is faster when it is hotter, when there is more wind, and when the water has a larger surface exposed to the air. Evaporation happens all the time around us, even without boiling.
topics.easyExplanation: Evaporation is when water slowly turns into invisible vapour and floats into the air, like wet clothes drying. It happens faster in heat, wind and open spaces.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See water disappear
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Wet clothes dry and puddles vanish because the water seems to disappear.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The liquid water changes into an invisible gas called water vapour and mixes with the air.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name it evaporation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This slow change of water from liquid to vapour at any temperature is called evaporation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note what speeds it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Evaporation is faster with more heat, more wind and a larger exposed surface.

examples: Wet clothes dry on a sunny, windy day because of evaporation.
Water spread on the floor dries faster than the same water in a narrow glass.

importantKeywords.definition: The slow change of water from liquid to vapour at any temperature.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A drying puddle shows evaporation.

importantKeywords.definition: Water in the form of an invisible gas.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water vapour rises from a wet road in the Sun.

importantKeywords.definition: The amount of surface exposed to air.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Spread-out water has more surface area and dries faster.

revisionNotes: Evaporation: liquid water -> water vapour.

topics.meaning: Condensation is the change of water vapour (gas) back into liquid water when it is cooled.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sometimes water droplets appear on the outside of a cold glass or bottle, even though no one poured water on it. This water comes from the invisible water vapour present in the air. When the vapour touches the cold surface, it cools down and changes back into tiny drops of liquid water. This change of water vapour into liquid water on cooling is called condensation. Condensation is the opposite of evaporation, and it is the reason we see dew and fog, and droplets on a cold mirror.
topics.easyExplanation: Condensation is when invisible water vapour in the air cools and turns back into liquid drops, like the water on a cold glass. It is the opposite of evaporation.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water drops appear on the outside of a cold glass without anyone adding water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand cooling
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When the vapour touches the cold surface, it cools and turns into tiny liquid drops.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name it condensation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This change of vapour into liquid water on cooling is called condensation, the opposite of evaporation.

examples: Water droplets form on a cold water bottle taken out of the fridge.
Dew on grass in the early morning forms by condensation of water vapour.

topics.meaning: Water exists in three states: solid (ice), liquid (water) and gas (water vapour).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Water is special because it can be found around us in all three states of matter. As a solid, water is ice, which has a fixed shape, like in an ice cube or snow. As a liquid, it is the water we drink, which flows and takes the shape of its container. As a gas, it is water vapour, which is invisible and spreads in the air. Water can change from one state to another when it is heated or cooled, which is why water keeps moving between these states in nature.
topics.easyExplanation: Water comes in three forms: ice (solid), water (liquid) and water vapour (gas). It changes between them when heated or cooled.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solid state
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: As ice, water is a solid with a fixed shape, like an ice cube or snow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Liquid state
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: As water, it flows and takes the shape of its container.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Gaseous state
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: As water vapour, it is an invisible gas that spreads in the air.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Changing states
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Heating and cooling change water from one state to another.

examples: Ice in a glass (solid) melts into water (liquid) and can boil into steam (gas).
Snow on mountains is the solid state of water.

importantKeywords.definition: The solid state of water.

importantKeywords.definition: The form matter takes: solid, liquid or gas.

topics.meaning: Water changes state through melting, freezing, evaporation (boiling) and condensation, on heating or cooling.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Water changes from one state to another when heat is added or removed. When ice is heated, it melts into liquid water; this is called melting. When liquid water is cooled enough, it freezes into ice; this is called freezing. When water is heated more, it changes into vapour by evaporation, and rapidly by boiling. When water vapour is cooled, it changes back into liquid water by condensation. So heating moves water towards the gas state, and cooling moves it towards the solid state.
topics.easyExplanation: Heating melts ice to water and boils water to vapour; cooling freezes water to ice and condenses vapour to water. Heat adds; cooling removes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Melting
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: On heating, ice changes into liquid water. This is melting.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Freezing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: On cooling, liquid water changes into ice. This is freezing.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Evaporation/boiling
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: On heating further, water changes into vapour, slowly by evaporation and quickly by boiling.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Condensation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: On cooling, water vapour changes back into liquid water. This is condensation.

examples: An ice cream melts into liquid on a hot day (melting).
Water kept in a freezer turns into ice (freezing).

importantKeywords.definition: The change of a solid into a liquid on heating.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Ice melting into water is melting.

importantKeywords.definition: The change of a liquid into a solid on cooling.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water turning to ice is freezing.

importantKeywords.definition: The rapid change of a liquid into vapour on strong heating.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water boiling into steam at 100 degree C.

topics.quickSummary: Water changes state by melting (ice to water), freezing (water to ice), evaporation or boiling (water to vapour) and condensation (vapour to water), driven by heating and cooling.
revisionNotes: Heating: ice -> water (melt) -> vapour (boil/evaporate).
Cooling: vapour -> water (condense) -> ice (freeze).
Heat added moves toward gas.

topics.meaning: The rate of evaporation increases with more heat, more wind, lower humidity and a larger exposed surface.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Evaporation can be made faster or slower by changing certain conditions. Evaporation is faster when the temperature is higher, so clothes dry quickly on a hot day. It is faster when there is more wind, because the wind carries away the water vapour. It is faster when the air is dry (low humidity), and when the water has a larger surface exposed to the air. On the other hand, evaporation is slower on cold, calm and humid days. Understanding this helps us, for example, dry clothes and crops more quickly.
topics.easyExplanation: Water evaporates faster when it is hot, windy, dry and spread out, and slower when it is cold, still and humid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Heat speeds it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Higher temperature makes water evaporate faster, so clothes dry quickly in the Sun.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Wind speeds it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Moving air carries away vapour, so windy days speed up evaporation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dry air speeds it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When the air is dry (low humidity), water evaporates faster.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Surface area speeds it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Spreading water over a larger surface makes it evaporate faster.

examples: Clothes spread out on a windy, sunny day dry much faster.
Tea cools faster when poured into a saucer because of its larger surface.

importantKeywords.definition: How fast evaporation happens.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A hot day increases the rate of evaporation.

importantKeywords.definition: The amount of water vapour in the air.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: On humid days, washing dries slowly.

importantKeywords.definition: Moving air that carries away water vapour.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Wind speeds up the drying of clothes.

topics.quickSummary: Evaporation is faster with more heat, more wind, drier air and a larger exposed surface, and slower in cold, calm and humid conditions.
revisionNotes: Faster evaporation: more heat, wind, dryness, surface area.
Slower: cold, calm, humid.
Wind carries vapour away.
Spread-out water evaporates faster.

topics.meaning: Evaporation causes cooling because it takes away heat from its surroundings.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When water evaporates, it absorbs heat from whatever it is in contact with, making that surface cooler. This is called the cooling effect of evaporation. This is why we feel cool when sweat evaporates from our skin, and why water stored in an earthen pot (matka) stays cool, as water seeping through its tiny pores evaporates and cools the pot. The same effect is why we feel cold when we step out of a swimming pool into the wind. Evaporation taking away heat is a natural way of cooling.
topics.easyExplanation: When water evaporates, it pulls heat away and cools things down. That is why sweating cools us and water in a clay pot stays cool.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Evaporation needs heat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To change into vapour, water absorbs heat from its surroundings.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Surroundings cool down
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because heat is taken away, the surface in contact becomes cooler.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the cooling effect
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: This cooling caused by evaporation is called the cooling effect.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See it in daily life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sweat evaporating cools the body, and an earthen pot keeps water cool by evaporation.

examples: Sweat evaporating from our skin cools us down on a hot day.
Water in a clay pot (matka) stays cool because water evaporating from its surface takes away heat.

importantKeywords.definition: The cooling caused when water evaporates and takes away heat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sweating cools us by the cooling effect.

importantKeywords.definition: The salty liquid released by the body that cools us as it evaporates.

importantKeywords.definition: A clay pot that keeps water cool by evaporation.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A matka keeps drinking water cool in summer.

topics.topic: How do Clouds give us Rain? (The Water Cycle)
topics.meaning: Water moves continuously between the Earth and the sky through evaporation, condensation and precipitation, called the water cycle.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The water from oceans, rivers and lakes is heated by the Sun and evaporates into water vapour, which rises up into the sky. As the vapour rises higher, it cools and condenses into tiny water droplets that together form clouds. When these droplets join to become larger and heavier, they fall down as rain, snow or hail; this is called precipitation. The rainwater flows back into rivers, lakes and oceans, and the whole process repeats. This continuous movement of water between the Earth and the sky is called the water cycle.
topics.easyExplanation: The Sun evaporates water into vapour, which rises, cools into clouds, and falls back as rain. This endless journey of water is the water cycle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Evaporation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Sun heats water in oceans and rivers, turning it into vapour that rises up.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Condensation into clouds
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: High in the cooler sky, the vapour condenses into tiny droplets that form clouds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Precipitation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When the droplets grow large and heavy, they fall as rain, snow or hail.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Back to start
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The water flows back into rivers and oceans, and the cycle repeats endlessly.

examples: Rain falls when cloud droplets join to become heavy enough to drop.
Water evaporating from the sea eventually returns as rain over land.

importantKeywords.definition: The continuous movement of water between the Earth and the sky.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rain returning to rivers is part of the water cycle.

chapters.chapter: Methods of Separation in Everyday Life

topics.meaning: We separate substances to remove useless or harmful parts, to keep useful parts, or to obtain pure substances.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Many things we use are mixtures of two or more substances, and we often need to separate them. We separate substances for several reasons: to remove unwanted or harmful materials (like stones from rice), to separate useful components that are mixed together (like cream from milk), or to obtain a pure substance (like salt from seawater). The method we choose for separation depends on the nature of the substances in the mixture, such as their size, weight, or whether they dissolve in water.
topics.easyExplanation: We separate mixtures to throw out the bad bits, keep the useful bits, or get something pure. The method depends on what is mixed together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognise mixtures
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many materials are mixtures of two or more substances mixed together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Remove the unwanted
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We separate to remove harmful or useless parts, like stones from rice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Keep the useful
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We separate to take out useful components, like butter from curd.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose the method
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The separation method depends on the properties of the substances, like size, weight or solubility.

examples: Removing small stones from rice before cooking is a separation.
Getting salt from seawater is separating a useful substance from a mixture.

importantKeywords.definition: A material made of two or more substances mixed together.

importantKeywords.definition: The process of taking apart the substances in a mixture.

topics.quickSummary: We separate substances to remove harmful or useless parts, to keep useful parts, or to obtain pure substances; the method chosen depends on the substances' properties.

topics.meaning: Handpicking is the method of separating slightly larger impurities from a mixture by simply picking them out with the hand.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Handpicking is one of the simplest methods of separation. It is used when the substances to be separated are present in small quantities and are large enough to be seen and picked up by hand, and are different in size, colour or shape from the rest. For example, we pick out small stones, husk or insects from grains like rice and wheat. Handpicking works well only when there are a few impurities to remove and they are big enough to handle.
topics.easyExplanation: Handpicking just means picking out the bad bits by hand, like removing stones from rice. It works when the bits are few and big enough to see.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Spot the impurities
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Look for pieces that differ in size, colour or shape, like stones in rice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick them out
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use your hand to pick out these unwanted pieces from the mixture.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know when it works
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Handpicking suits mixtures with only a few impurities that are large enough to handle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Get a cleaner mixture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After handpicking, the useful grains are left free of the unwanted pieces.

examples: Removing small stones and husk from rice by hand before cooking.
Picking out spoiled or discoloured grains from a handful of pulses.

importantKeywords.definition: Separating large impurities from a mixture by hand.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Removing stones from wheat is handpicking.

importantKeywords.definition: An unwanted substance mixed in with a useful one.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A stone in rice is an impurity.

topics.quickSummary: Handpicking separates a few large, visible impurities, like stones from grains, by picking them out by hand; it works only when impurities are few and big enough.
revisionNotes: Handpicking = picking out impurities by hand.

topics.meaning: Threshing is the method of separating grain seeds from their stalks by beating the harvested crop.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After crops like wheat are harvested, the grain seeds are still attached to the dried stalks. Threshing is the process of beating the stalks to loosen and separate the grain from them. It can be done by hand by striking the stalks against a hard surface, by using animals to walk over them, or by machines called threshers. Threshing is an important step in farming, carried out after harvesting and before the grain can be cleaned and stored.
topics.easyExplanation: Threshing means beating harvested crops to knock the grain seeds off their dried stalks. Farmers do it by hand, with animals, or with machines.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Harvest the crop
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Crops are cut, but the grains remain stuck to the dried stalks.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Beat the stalks
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The stalks are beaten so that the grain seeds loosen and fall off.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use different ways
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Threshing can be done by hand, by animals walking over the crop, or by a thresher machine.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prepare for cleaning
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After threshing, the grain is separated from the stalks and ready for further cleaning.

examples: Farmers beat bundles of wheat to separate the grains from the stalks.
A threshing machine quickly separates grain from straw on large farms.

importantKeywords.definition: Separating grain seeds from stalks by beating.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Beating wheat stalks to free the grain is threshing.

importantKeywords.definition: A machine used for threshing crops.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A thresher separates grain from straw quickly.

topics.quickSummary: Threshing separates grain seeds from their dried stalks by beating, done by hand, with animals, or by machines, as a key step after harvesting.
revisionNotes: Threshing = beating crops to free grain from stalks.

Methods: by hand, animals, or thresher machine.
