topics.meaning: Winnowing is the method of separating lighter husk from heavier grain using wind or by blowing air.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: After threshing, the grain is still mixed with light husk. Winnowing is used to separate this lighter husk from the heavier grain. In winnowing, the mixture is dropped from a height in the presence of wind or a stream of air. The lighter husk is carried away by the wind and falls a little distance away, while the heavier grain falls straight down and collects in a heap below. Winnowing is commonly used by farmers to clean grains and works because of the difference in weight between the two components.
topics.easyExplanation: Winnowing uses wind to separate light husk from heavy grain. You drop the mixture and the wind blows the light husk away while heavy grain falls down.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Have the mixture ready
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After threshing, the grain is still mixed with light husk.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Drop it in wind
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pour the mixture from a height while wind or a fan blows across it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Wind separates them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The lighter husk is blown away while the heavier grain falls straight down.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Collect the grain
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The clean, heavier grain collects in a heap directly below.

examples: Farmers drop threshed grain from a basket in the wind so the husk blows away.
Light chaff is separated from heavier seeds by winnowing on a breezy day.

importantKeywords.definition: Separating lighter and heavier components using wind.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Separating husk from grain by wind is winnowing.

importantKeywords.definition: The light, dry outer covering of grain.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Husk is blown away during winnowing.

importantKeywords.definition: The light waste separated from grain.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Chaff is carried away by the wind.

topics.quickSummary: Winnowing separates lighter husk from heavier grain by dropping the mixture in wind; the husk blows away while the grain falls straight down.
revisionNotes: Winnowing uses wind to separate husk and grain.
Lighter husk is blown away.
Heavier grain falls straight down.
Works due to weight difference.

topics.meaning: Sieving is the method of separating components of different sizes using a sieve with small holes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Sieving is used to separate a mixture of substances that have different particle sizes, when one is fine enough to pass through the holes of a sieve and the other is too big. The mixture is placed on a sieve and shaken; the smaller particles pass through the holes while the larger particles stay on top. Sieving is commonly used to separate fine flour from larger husk or bran, or to separate small stones and pebbles from sand. It works because of the difference in the size of the particles.
topics.easyExplanation: Sieving separates things by size using a net with holes. Small bits fall through and big bits stay on top, like sifting flour to remove lumps.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Have differently sized particles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sieving works when components differ in size, like fine flour and coarse bran.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use a sieve
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place the mixture on a sieve, which has small holes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Shake it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shake the sieve so the smaller particles fall through the holes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate by size
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The larger particles stay on top while the finer ones pass through.

examples: Sieving wheat flour to separate fine flour from the coarse bran.
Builders sieve sand to remove small pebbles before using it.

importantKeywords.definition: Separating particles of different sizes using a sieve.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sifting flour to remove lumps is sieving.

importantKeywords.definition: A tool with small holes used to separate particles by size.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A kitchen sieve separates fine flour from bran.

topics.quickSummary: Sieving separates components of different sizes using a sieve: small particles pass through the holes while larger ones remain on top.
revisionNotes: Sieving separates particles by size.
Uses a sieve with small holes.
Small particles pass; large stay on top.
Example: fine flour from bran.

topics.meaning: Sedimentation is letting heavier insoluble particles settle, and decantation is pouring off the clear liquid above them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a mixture of an insoluble solid and a liquid, like muddy water, is left undisturbed, the heavier solid particles slowly settle down at the bottom. This settling of heavier particles is called sedimentation, and the settled layer is called the sediment. After sedimentation, the clear liquid above can be gently poured into another container without disturbing the settled solid; this pouring off of the clear liquid is called decantation. These two methods are often used together to separate insoluble solids from liquids, such as cleaning muddy water.
topics.easyExplanation: Let muddy water sit and the mud sinks to the bottom (sedimentation). Then gently pour off the clear water on top (decantation).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Leave the mixture still
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Let a mixture like muddy water stand undisturbed for some time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sedimentation occurs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The heavier insoluble particles settle down at the bottom as sediment.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decant the liquid
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Gently pour off the clear liquid on top into another container.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate solid and liquid
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Together, sedimentation and decantation separate insoluble solids from liquids.

examples: Muddy water left to stand has mud settling at the bottom, and clear water can be poured off.
Sand mixed in water settles down, allowing the clear water above to be decanted.

importantKeywords.definition: The settling of heavier insoluble particles at the bottom of a liquid.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mud settling in still water is sedimentation.

importantKeywords.definition: Pouring off the clear liquid above the settled solid.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Pouring clear water off settled mud is decantation.

importantKeywords.definition: The solid that settles at the bottom.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mud at the bottom of a glass is sediment.

topics.quickSummary: Sedimentation lets heavier insoluble particles settle at the bottom, and decantation pours off the clear liquid above; together they separate insoluble solids from liquids.
revisionNotes: Sedimentation = heavier particles settle down.
Settled solid = sediment.
Decantation = pouring off the clear liquid.
Used together to clean muddy water.

topics.meaning: Filtration is the method of separating insoluble solids from a liquid by passing the mixture through a filter.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Filtration is used to separate insoluble solid particles from a liquid, especially when the particles are small and do not settle easily. The mixture is poured through a filter, such as a filter paper or a clean cloth, which has tiny pores. The liquid passes through the pores and is collected as the clear filtrate, while the solid particles are trapped on the filter as the residue. Filtration gives a cleaner separation than decantation because even fine particles are caught. We use filtration when we strain tea or filter drinking water.
topics.easyExplanation: Filtration pours a mixture through a filter that traps the solids and lets the liquid pass, like straining tea leaves out of tea.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Have a solid-liquid mixture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use filtration when small insoluble solids are mixed in a liquid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pour through a filter
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pass the mixture through a filter paper or cloth with tiny pores.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Liquid passes through
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The clear liquid, called the filtrate, passes through and is collected.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solid is trapped
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The solid particles, called the residue, stay on the filter.

examples: Straining tea through a strainer keeps the tea leaves back and lets the tea pass.
Filtering muddy water through a cloth traps the mud and gives cleaner water.

importantKeywords.definition: Separating insoluble solids from a liquid using a filter.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Straining tea is filtration.

importantKeywords.definition: The clear liquid that passes through the filter.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The tea collected below the strainer is the filtrate.

importantKeywords.definition: The solid left behind on the filter.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Tea leaves on the strainer are the residue.

topics.quickSummary: Filtration separates insoluble solids from a liquid by passing the mixture through a filter: the liquid passes as filtrate while the solid stays as residue.
revisionNotes: Filtration uses a filter with tiny pores.
Liquid passes through = filtrate.
Solid trapped on filter = residue.
Cleaner than decantation for fine particles.

topics.meaning: Evaporation can separate a dissolved soluble solid from a liquid by evaporating the liquid away.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a solid is dissolved in a liquid, like salt in water, it cannot be separated by filtration because the dissolved particles pass through the filter. Instead, we use evaporation. The solution is heated or left in the Sun so that the liquid (water) slowly evaporates as vapour, leaving the dissolved solid behind. This is how salt is obtained from seawater: the seawater is collected in shallow pans and the Sun's heat evaporates the water, leaving salt behind. Evaporation is used to separate a soluble solid from its solution.
topics.easyExplanation: To get a dissolved solid back, like salt from salty water, we evaporate the water away and the solid is left behind. That is how sea salt is made.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Have a solution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use evaporation when a soluble solid is dissolved in a liquid, like salt in water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Heat or sun-dry it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Heat the solution or leave it in the Sun so the liquid slowly evaporates.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Liquid evaporates
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The water changes into vapour and escapes into the air.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solid is left behind
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The dissolved solid, like salt, remains behind once the water has gone.

examples: Salt is obtained from seawater by evaporating the water in shallow pans in the Sun.
Heating salt water in a dish until the water dries leaves salt behind.

importantKeywords.definition: Changing a liquid into vapour to leave a dissolved solid behind.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Drying salt water to get salt uses evaporation.

importantKeywords.definition: A mixture in which a solid is fully dissolved in a liquid.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Salt water is a solution.

importantKeywords.definition: Able to dissolve in a liquid.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sugar is soluble in water.

topics.quickSummary: Evaporation separates a dissolved soluble solid from a liquid by evaporating the liquid away, leaving the solid behind, as when salt is obtained from seawater.
revisionNotes: Dissolved solids can't be filtered out.
Evaporation removes the liquid as vapour.
The soluble solid is left behind.
Used to get salt from seawater.

topics.topic: What Sets the Living Apart from the Non-living
topics.meaning: Living things show characteristics like growth, breathing, needing food, responding to surroundings, reproducing and excreting, which non-living things do not.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can tell living things apart from non-living things by certain features called characteristics of living beings. Living things grow in size, they need food for energy, they breathe (respire), they respond to changes around them (stimuli), they reproduce to give rise to young ones, they get rid of waste (excretion), and they move on their own. Non-living things, like a stone or a chair, do not show these features. However, we must look at several characteristics together, because some non-living things may show one feature, like a car that moves, but not the others.
topics.easyExplanation: Living things grow, eat, breathe, react, reproduce and remove waste. Non-living things like rocks do not. We check several features together to be sure.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Growth and food
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living things grow over time and need food for energy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Respiration and response
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living things breathe (respire) and respond to changes in their surroundings called stimuli.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reproduction and excretion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living things reproduce to produce young ones and remove waste through excretion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare with non-living
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Non-living things show none of these together, though one feature alone (like movement) is not enough to call something living.

examples: A plant grows, makes food, breathes and reproduces, so it is living.
A car moves but does not grow, eat or reproduce, so it is non-living.

importantKeywords.definition: A change in surroundings that a living thing responds to.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Light is a stimulus that makes a plant bend towards it.

importantKeywords.definition: The process of breathing to release energy from food.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: We breathe in oxygen during respiration.

importantKeywords.definition: Removing waste from the body.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Plants and animals remove waste by excretion.

topics.quickSummary: Living things show growth, need for food, respiration, response to stimuli, reproduction and excretion, which non-living things do not; several features must be seen together.
revisionNotes: Living things: grow, feed, respire, respond, reproduce, excrete.
Non-living things lack these features.
Response to stimulus is a key sign of life.
Check several characteristics together, not just one.

topics.meaning: Germination is the sprouting of a seed into a new plant, and it needs water, air and a suitable temperature.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A seed can grow into a new plant through a process called germination, in which the seed sprouts and a tiny plant comes out. For germination to happen, a seed needs certain conditions: water, which softens the seed and starts growth; air, which provides oxygen for the seed to respire; and a suitable (warm) temperature. If any of these is missing, the seed will not germinate. This is why dry seeds stored in a packet do not sprout, but the same seeds grow when planted in moist, warm soil with air.
topics.easyExplanation: Germination is a seed sprouting into a baby plant. For this it needs water, air and the right warmth. Without these, the seed stays asleep.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand germination
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Germination is the process in which a seed sprouts and grows into a new plant.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water softens the seed and triggers the start of growth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide air
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Air gives the seed oxygen, which it needs to respire and grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Provide warmth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A suitable, warm temperature is needed; if water, air or warmth is missing, the seed will not germinate.

examples: Soaked gram seeds kept on moist cotton sprout in a few days.
Dry seeds in a sealed packet do not grow because they lack water.

importantKeywords.definition: The sprouting of a seed into a new plant.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A bean seed sprouting in wet soil is germination.

importantKeywords.definition: The part of a plant from which a new plant grows.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A mango seed can grow into a mango tree.

importantKeywords.definition: The first tiny growth that comes out of a seed.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A green sprout appears from a germinating seed.

topics.quickSummary: Germination is the sprouting of a seed into a new plant, and it needs three conditions: water, air and a suitable warm temperature.
revisionNotes: Germination = seed sprouting into a new plant.
Needs water, air and suitable temperature.
Water softens the seed; air gives oxygen.
Missing any condition stops germination.

topics.meaning: Plants grow and also show movement, such as bending towards light or closing their leaves, in response to stimuli.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Although plants are fixed in the ground and cannot move from place to place, they still grow and show movements in response to changes around them. Plant parts grow and move in response to stimuli like light, water and touch. For example, the shoot of a plant bends and grows towards light, and roots grow downwards towards water and gravity. The leaves of the touch-me-not (chhui-mui) plant fold up when touched. These responses show that plants are living and sensitive to their surroundings, even though their movements are slow.
topics.easyExplanation: Plants can't walk, but they grow and move slowly in response to things, like a stem bending towards light or a touch-me-not folding when touched.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plants are fixed but active
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants cannot move from place to place but still grow and show movements.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Respond to light
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shoots bend and grow towards light, which helps them make food.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Respond to water and gravity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Roots grow downwards towards water and the pull of gravity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Respond to touch
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The touch-me-not plant folds its leaves when touched, showing sensitivity to stimuli.

examples: A potted plant on a windowsill bends towards the sunlight.
The leaves of a touch-me-not plant close quickly when touched.

importantKeywords.definition: A change that causes a response in a living thing.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Light is a stimulus for a plant shoot.

importantKeywords.definition: The part of a plant that grows upwards, including the stem and leaves.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A shoot bends towards light.

importantKeywords.definition: The reaction of a living thing to a stimulus.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A plant bending to light is a response.

topics.quickSummary: Plants, though fixed, grow and show slow movements in response to stimuli like light, water, gravity and touch, showing they are living and sensitive.
revisionNotes: Plants are fixed but grow and move slowly.
Shoots bend towards light.
Roots grow towards water and gravity.
Touch-me-not folds leaves when touched.

topics.meaning: The life cycle of a plant is the journey from a seed to a mature plant that produces new seeds.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every flowering plant goes through a life cycle. It begins as a seed, which germinates into a tiny seedling. The seedling grows into a young plant and then into a mature, adult plant with roots, stem and leaves. The mature plant produces flowers, which develop into fruits containing new seeds. These seeds can then grow into new plants, and the cycle continues. In this way, plants reproduce and their kind continues from one generation to the next.
topics.easyExplanation: A plant's life cycle goes: seed -> seedling -> grown plant -> flowers -> fruits with new seeds, and then the new seeds start the cycle again.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start as a seed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The life cycle begins with a seed, which germinates into a seedling.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Grow into a plant
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The seedling grows into a young plant and then a mature plant with roots, stem and leaves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Produce flowers and fruits
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The mature plant grows flowers, which turn into fruits containing new seeds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Continue the cycle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The new seeds can grow into new plants, repeating the life cycle.

examples: A bean seed grows into a plant that flowers and produces new bean seeds.
A sunflower grows from a seed, blooms, and forms seeds that grow into new sunflowers.

importantKeywords.definition: The series of changes from birth to producing new young ones.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A plant's life cycle runs from seed to new seed.

importantKeywords.definition: A young plant that has just sprouted from a seed.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A small seedling grows from a germinated seed.

importantKeywords.definition: The part of a plant that helps it reproduce and form seeds.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A flower develops into a fruit with seeds.

topics.quickSummary: A plant's life cycle runs from seed to seedling to mature plant, which produces flowers and fruits with new seeds that grow into new plants, continuing the cycle.
revisionNotes: Life cycle: seed -> seedling -> mature plant.
Mature plant produces flowers and fruits.
Fruits contain new seeds.
New seeds restart the cycle.

topics.meaning: The life cycle of an animal is the journey from birth to becoming an adult that can produce young ones.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Animals also have life cycles, but they differ from one animal to another. Some animals, like humans, dogs and cows, give birth to young ones that look like the adults and simply grow bigger. Others, like frogs and insects, change form completely as they grow. For example, a frog begins as an egg, hatches into a tadpole that lives in water and has a tail, and then gradually changes into an adult frog; this change of form is seen in their life cycle. A butterfly's life cycle goes from egg to larva (caterpillar) to pupa to adult butterfly. In every case, the adult can produce new young ones, continuing the cycle.
topics.easyExplanation: Animals grow through life cycles. Some babies look like their parents and just get bigger; others, like frogs and butterflies, change shape completely as they grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Begin life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An animal's life cycle starts with birth or hatching from an egg.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Grow up
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some young, like puppies, look like adults and grow bigger; others change form as they grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change of form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A frog goes egg -> tadpole -> adult frog, and a butterfly goes egg -> caterpillar -> pupa -> butterfly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reproduce
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The adult animal produces new young ones, continuing the life cycle.

examples: A frog's life cycle: egg -> tadpole -> adult frog.
A butterfly's life cycle: egg -> caterpillar -> pupa -> butterfly.

importantKeywords.definition: The stages an animal passes through from birth to adulthood and reproduction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A frog's life cycle includes the tadpole stage.

importantKeywords.definition: The young, water-living stage of a frog with a tail.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A tadpole grows into a frog.

importantKeywords.definition: An early, often worm-like stage of some animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A caterpillar is the larva of a butterfly.

topics.quickSummary: Animals pass through life cycles from birth to adulthood; some young resemble adults and grow, while others like frogs and butterflies change form, and adults produce new young.
revisionNotes: Animal life cycle: birth -> growth -> adult -> young.
Some young look like adults (e.g., puppies).
Frog: egg -> tadpole -> adult frog.
Butterfly: egg -> caterpillar -> pupa -> butterfly.

topics.meaning: Air is a natural resource, a mixture of gases that surrounds the Earth and is essential for life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Air is one of nature's most important treasures. It is a mixture of gases, mainly nitrogen and oxygen, along with small amounts of carbon dioxide, water vapour and other gases. The blanket of air surrounding the Earth is called the atmosphere. Air is essential for life: living things need oxygen from the air to breathe, and plants need carbon dioxide to make food. Air is also used to fly kites and aeroplanes, to run windmills, and moving air (wind) is a source of energy. We must keep our air clean and prevent air pollution.
topics.easyExplanation: Air is a mixture of gases all around us. We need its oxygen to breathe and plants need its carbon dioxide. Moving air can even give us energy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Air is a mixture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Air is a mixture of gases, mainly nitrogen and oxygen, plus carbon dioxide, water vapour and others.

stepByStepExplanation.title: It forms the atmosphere
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The layer of air around the Earth is called the atmosphere.

stepByStepExplanation.title: It supports life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living things need oxygen to breathe and plants need carbon dioxide to make food.

stepByStepExplanation.title: It is useful and must be kept clean
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Air helps fly kites and planes and runs windmills, and we must prevent air pollution.

examples: We breathe in oxygen from the air to stay alive.
A windmill uses moving air to grind grain or generate electricity.

importantKeywords.definition: The blanket of air surrounding the Earth.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The atmosphere protects life on Earth.

importantKeywords.definition: The gas in air that living things need to breathe.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: We take in oxygen when we breathe.

importantKeywords.definition: The dirtying of air with harmful substances.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Smoke from vehicles causes air pollution.

topics.quickSummary: Air is a natural resource and a mixture of gases (mainly nitrogen and oxygen) forming the atmosphere; it is essential for breathing and plant food, useful in many ways, and must be kept clean.
revisionNotes: Air is a mixture of gases (mainly nitrogen, oxygen).
Layer of air = atmosphere.
Oxygen for breathing; carbon dioxide for plant food.
Keep air clean; prevent air pollution.

topics.meaning: Water is a precious natural resource needed by all living things, and most of the Earth's water is in the oceans.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Water is a vital natural treasure that all living things need to survive. We use water for drinking, cooking, cleaning, farming and many industries. Although the Earth has a lot of water, most of it is salty water in the oceans, and only a small part is fresh water found in rivers, lakes and underground that we can use. Because usable fresh water is limited, we must use it carefully and avoid wasting and polluting it. Saving water and preventing water pollution are important duties for everyone.
topics.easyExplanation: Water is precious and needed by all life. Most of Earth's water is salty sea water; only a little is fresh water we can use, so we must save it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water is essential
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: All living things need water to survive, and we use it for drinking, cooking, farming and industry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Most water is salty
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Although the Earth has plenty of water, most of it is salty ocean water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fresh water is limited
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only a small part is usable fresh water in rivers, lakes and underground.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Save and protect it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because fresh water is limited, we must use it carefully and avoid wasting or polluting it.

examples: We use water every day for drinking, bathing and cooking.
Farmers need large amounts of water to irrigate their crops.

importantKeywords.definition: Water that is not salty and can be used by us.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: River water is fresh water.

importantKeywords.definition: The dirtying of water with harmful substances.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Throwing waste into rivers causes water pollution.

importantKeywords.definition: Using a resource carefully to avoid wasting it.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Turning off taps is water conservation.

topics.quickSummary: Water is a precious natural resource needed by all living things; most of Earth's water is salty, only a little is usable fresh water, so we must conserve it and prevent pollution.
revisionNotes: Water is essential for all life.
Most of Earth's water is salty (oceans).
Only a small part is usable fresh water.
Conserve water; prevent water pollution.

topics.meaning: The Sun is the main source of energy for the Earth, giving light and heat that support almost all life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Sun is the most important source of energy for the Earth. It gives us light and heat, without which there would be no life. Green plants use sunlight to make their food through photosynthesis, and since animals depend on plants, almost all life depends on the Sun's energy. The Sun also drives the water cycle and the winds. Solar energy, the energy from the Sun, is a clean and renewable source of energy that can be used to heat water and generate electricity using solar panels.
topics.easyExplanation: The Sun gives us light and heat. Plants use it to make food, so almost all life depends on the Sun. We can also turn sunlight into electricity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sun gives light and heat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Sun is the main source of light and heat energy for the Earth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plants use sunlight
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Green plants use sunlight to make their food, and animals depend on plants.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sun drives nature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Sun's energy powers the water cycle and the winds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use solar energy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Solar energy is clean and renewable, used to heat water and make electricity with solar panels.

examples: A solar panel uses sunlight to produce electricity.
Plants make their food using sunlight, supporting all the animals that eat them.

importantKeywords.definition: Energy obtained from the Sun.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A solar cooker uses solar energy to cook food.

importantKeywords.definition: The process by which plants make food using sunlight.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Leaves carry out photosynthesis.

importantKeywords.definition: Energy from a source that does not run out.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Sunlight is a renewable energy source.

topics.quickSummary: The Sun is the Earth's main energy source, giving light and heat that support plant food-making and nearly all life; solar energy is a clean, renewable resource.
revisionNotes: The Sun is the main source of energy.
Plants use sunlight to make food.
Almost all life depends on the Sun.
Solar energy is clean and renewable.

topics.meaning: Forests are large areas covered with trees and other plants, and they are a valuable natural resource.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Forests are large areas of land thickly covered with trees, plants and home to many animals. They are one of nature's greatest treasures. Forests give us many useful things such as wood, fruits, medicines, gum and many raw materials. More importantly, forests release oxygen and absorb carbon dioxide, helping to keep the air clean, and they provide a home (habitat) to countless plants and animals. Forests also prevent soil erosion, help bring rainfall, and keep the environment cool. Because forests are so valuable, we must protect them and not cut down trees carelessly.
topics.easyExplanation: Forests are big areas full of trees and animals. They give us wood, fruits and medicines, clean our air, and are home to wildlife, so we must protect them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What forests are
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forests are large areas covered with trees and plants and full of animals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: They give resources
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forests provide wood, fruits, medicines, gum and many raw materials.

stepByStepExplanation.title: They protect the environment
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forests release oxygen, absorb carbon dioxide, prevent soil erosion and help bring rain.

stepByStepExplanation.title: They are homes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Forests are the habitat of countless plants and animals, so we must protect them.

examples: Forests give us timber, medicines and many fruits.
Many animals like tigers, deer and birds live in forests.

importantKeywords.definition: A large area covered with trees, plants and animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A tiger lives in a forest.

importantKeywords.definition: The cutting down of forests.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Deforestation harms wildlife and the environment.

importantKeywords.definition: The natural home of plants and animals.

topics.meaning: Soil, rocks and minerals are natural resources from the Earth that support plants and provide useful materials.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The upper layer of the Earth that supports plant life is called soil. Soil is formed over a very long time by the breaking down of rocks and is essential for farming, as plants grow in it and get water and nutrients from it. Rocks are the hard, solid materials that make up the Earth's crust, and they come in many types. Minerals are useful natural substances obtained from the Earth, such as iron, copper, coal and salt, which we use to make metals, tools and many other products. These resources must be used wisely as they take a very long time to form.
topics.easyExplanation: Soil grows our plants and forms slowly from broken rocks. Rocks are hard Earth materials, and minerals like iron and coal are dug out to make useful things.

topics.meaning: Fossil fuels like coal, petroleum and natural gas are fuels formed from the remains of ancient plants and animals over millions of years.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Fossil fuels are an important source of energy formed from the buried remains of plants and animals that lived millions of years ago. Over a very long time, heat and pressure changed these remains into coal, petroleum (crude oil) and natural gas. We burn these fuels to run vehicles, generate electricity and cook food. However, fossil fuels take millions of years to form, so they are non-renewable; once used up, they cannot be replaced quickly. Burning them also causes pollution. Therefore, we should use fossil fuels carefully and look for cleaner alternatives.

topics.meaning: Natural resources are gifts of nature, classified as renewable (which can be replaced) and non-renewable (which cannot be replaced quickly).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Everything useful that we get from nature is called a natural resource, such as air, water, soil, sunlight, forests and minerals. Natural resources are of two main types. Renewable resources are those that can be replaced by nature in a short time and are not easily exhausted, like sunlight, air, water and forests. Non-renewable resources are those that take millions of years to form and can get used up, like coal, petroleum and minerals. We must use all natural resources wisely, especially the non-renewable ones, so that they last for the future.
topics.easyExplanation: Natural resources are nature's gifts. Renewable ones like sunlight and air keep coming back; non-renewable ones like coal can run out, so we must save them.

topics.meaning: Conservation means using natural resources carefully and avoiding waste so that they last for the future.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Since many natural resources are limited and some cannot be replaced, we must protect them through conservation. Conservation means using resources wisely, avoiding waste, and protecting them for future generations. We can conserve resources by saving water and electricity, reducing the use of fuels, reusing and recycling materials, planting trees, and not polluting air and water. Following the idea of 'Reduce, Reuse, Recycle' helps us save resources. Conserving nature's treasures is a responsibility shared by everyone so that future generations can also enjoy them.
topics.easyExplanation: Conservation means using resources carefully and not wasting them. We can save water and power, reuse and recycle, and plant trees to protect nature for the future.

topics.meaning: Stars are huge balls of hot, glowing gas, and a constellation is a group of stars forming a recognisable pattern in the sky.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we look at the night sky, we see countless tiny points of light called stars. Stars are huge balls of very hot, glowing gases that give out their own light and heat. They look tiny only because they are extremely far away. Some stars appear to form patterns or shapes in the sky, and such a group of stars forming a recognisable pattern is called a constellation. People in the past named constellations after animals, objects or characters, such as Ursa Major (the Great Bear, also called Saptarshi) and Orion. Constellations helped people find directions and tell the time at night.

topics.meaning: Watching the night sky helps us observe stars, planets and the Moon, and notice how they appear to move.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Observing the night sky is an exciting way to learn about objects beyond the Earth. On a clear, dark night, away from bright city lights, we can see many stars, some planets, and the Moon. We notice that the stars seem to move across the sky through the night; this is actually because the Earth is rotating. The Pole Star (Dhruv Tara) appears almost fixed in the north and can be located with the help of the Ursa Major constellation, which helps us find direction. Patient observation of the night sky has helped people learn a great deal about the universe.
topics.easyExplanation: On a clear, dark night you can spot stars, planets and the Moon. They seem to move because the Earth spins. The Pole Star stays fixed and shows north.

topics.meaning: The solar system is made up of the Sun and the eight planets, along with moons, asteroids and comets, that move around the Sun.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Our solar system is the family of the Sun and all the objects that move around it. At its centre is the Sun, a star, which holds everything together with its gravity. Eight planets move around the Sun in fixed paths called orbits; in order from the Sun they are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune. Many planets have moons that go around them, like the Moon goes around the Earth. The solar system also contains smaller bodies like asteroids and comets. The Earth is the only planet known to support life.

topics.meaning: A galaxy is a huge collection of billions of stars, and our solar system lies in a galaxy called the Milky Way.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Stars are not spread evenly across space; they are gathered into enormous groups called galaxies. A galaxy is a vast collection of billions of stars, along with gas and dust, held together by gravity. Our Sun and the entire solar system are part of a galaxy called the Milky Way, known in India as Akash Ganga. On a very dark, clear night, the Milky Way can be seen as a faint, milky band of light stretching across the sky, which is actually the combined light of its countless stars.

topics.meaning: The universe is everything that exists, including all galaxies, stars, planets and space itself.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The universe is the name we give to everything that exists: all the galaxies, stars, planets, moons, gas, dust and the vast space between them. It is unimaginably huge, containing billions of galaxies, each with billions of stars. Our Milky Way is just one of these countless galaxies. The universe is so large that we have explored only a tiny part of it. Scientists continue to study the universe using telescopes and space missions, and there is still a great deal left to discover, which is why space remains one of the most exciting areas of science.
